content.text: State Updates Are Asynchronous: Batching and Snapshots

immediately update the state variable and re-render. It schedules the update for later — specifically, after the current event handler (or effect) finishes running. This asynchronous behaviour enables two powerful optimisations:
(multiple updates collapse into one re-render) and
(each render captures a frozen copy of state). Understanding both is essential for writing correct React code.
Every time React renders your component, it passes the
into that render's execution. Those constants do not change for the lifetime of that render, no matter how many times you call the setter.

Think of each render as a photograph. The photo captures a fixed moment in time. Calling
doesn't alter the current photo — it orders a new photo to be taken.
The snapshot model trips up developers most often in asynchronous callbacks. The closure captures the snapshot, not a live reference.

attrs.alt: React batching diagram: three setState calls in one handler queued then flushed as a single re-render

content.text: Without batching, three state updates would cause three sequential re-renders, each triggering a DOM diff and commit. Batching reduces this to one, which is especially valuable in render-heavy trees.
Before React 18, batching only applied inside React event handlers (like
, or native DOM event listeners each triggered their own re-render. React 18 extended batching to cover all of these cases automatically.
Rarely, you need a state update to flush synchronously so that the DOM is updated before the next line runs — for example, to measure a DOM element immediately after updating the list it belongs to.
lets you do this at the cost of performance.

is an escape hatch. It hurts performance and forces React outside its normal batching model. Prefer it only when you truly need the DOM to reflect a state change synchronously before further JavaScript runs.
Multiple Updates to the Same State Variable
Calling the setter multiple times with a direct value in one handler is rarely what you intend — only the last call wins because each one replaces the queued value rather than stacking on it.

), React chains the updaters: the first updater receives the current state, and each subsequent updater receives the result of the previous one. The snapshot inside the event handler still doesn't change, but the queue accumulates correctly.
How React Processes the Update Queue
React processes the queue of updates after the event handler (or effect) exits. For each update in the queue:
If the update is a direct value (e.g.
If the update is an updater function (e.g.
), React calls the function with the current pending value and uses the return value.
After all updates in the queue are applied, React re-renders the component with the final value.
Practical Decision: Direct Value vs Updater Function

Multiple calls to the same setter
The snapshot model interacts with async code in a way that can produce stale reads. The updater function form sidesteps this for write operations, but for reads you need
Batching and State Reads in the Same Handler

Because the snapshot is frozen for the duration of the handler, reading state after calling the setter still returns the old value. This is expected behaviour — and a common source of bugs for developers new to React.
Seeing Batching in Action: Render Count
You can verify batching by counting renders with a ref.
Batching applies regardless of the type of state value. The key rule remains: never mutate the current snapshot — always produce a new value.
Reading state after setting it in the same handler.
The variable is frozen at the snapshot value. Move the logic to the render phase or derive it from the updated queue.
Calling the setter multiple times with a direct value expecting them to stack.
Only the last direct value wins. Use the updater function form to chain updates.
Expecting state to be fresh inside a setTimeout or Promise callback.
The closure captures the snapshot. Use a ref for readable live state or the updater form for writable state.
It is expensive and rarely necessary. Usually the correct fix is restructuring logic or using
wrapper ensures the update queue is flushed before assertions run. State updates triggered by events inside
State Updates and React's Render Phases
React's update cycle has two distinct phases, and understanding where batching fits helps clarify why the snapshot model exists:
Component functions run, JSX is evaluated, new virtual DOM produced
React applies DOM changes, runs refs, fires effects
Batching happens between events and the render phase: React collects all queued updates from event handlers and processes them before the render phase begins.
Putting It All Together: A Checkout Flow
This example demonstrates batching and the snapshot model working together in a realistic scenario.
— is batched by React 18, resulting in two re-renders total (loading, then success or error) rather than four.
Race Conditions and Stale Updates in Async Flows

When multiple async operations can overlap — for example, a search that fires a new request on each keystroke — an earlier request that resolves last can overwrite newer results. The snapshot model means you cannot rely on reading state inside the callback to know whether to discard the result.
changed), ensuring stale responses never call
. This pattern works because the closure captures the per-render
variable — each effect invocation has its own.
Transition Updates: Marking Low-Priority State Changes

to mark certain state updates as non-urgent. React can interrupt these transitions to prioritise more urgent updates (e.g. typing input), which keeps the UI responsive even during expensive re-renders.
update are still batched, but React is allowed to abandon the render triggered by
mid-flight if a higher-priority update (another keystroke) arrives. This is a key benefit of the asynchronous scheduling model.
When to Prefer useReducer Over Multiple useState Calls
When several state variables always change together and the next values depend on each other,
calls, because the reducer receives the complete current state and returns a complete next state atomically.
, the reducer sees both values together and computes the next state correctly every time.

React DevTools Profiler shows each render as a bar. When batching works correctly, multiple state updates within one event appear as a single short bar rather than several sequential ones. To verify batching in your components:
Open DevTools → Profiler tab → click the record button.
Click a button that calls multiple setters.
Stop recording and inspect the flame graph.
A single render bar confirms batching. Multiple bars for the same component in one event indicate an anti-pattern (calling setters outside React's batching boundary).
does not update the state variable immediately — it schedules an update and the current render's snapshot remains frozen.
calls within one event (or, in React 18, within one microtask) into a single re-render.
